intro_band.agent_remit_summary: An autonomous software-engineering agent that accepts natural-language tasks and completes them end-to-end — writing code, executing it inside a sandboxed runtime, browsing the web, and operating on connected version-control and issue-tracker integrations. Authorized counterparties are the operator, the configured LLM provider, scoped hosted-platform integrations, configured MCP tool servers, and the sandboxed runtime that serves as the agent's execution boundary; the public web is read-only via the browser tool and its returned content is untrusted. The remit declares hard rules — all agent-generated code MUST run inside the sandbox, file I/O MUST stay inside the per-session workspace, embedded instructions in fetched content MUST NOT be acted on, secrets MUST NOT be committed or logged, per-session step and time caps MUST be enforced, and destructive integration actions MUST be confirmed or refused outright — and a small set of approval-gated actions including cross-repo writes, PR merges, force-pushes, dependency-change commits, CI edits, and the addition of new MCP servers or integration accounts.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: This source snapshot is the OpenHands V1 app-server control plane — a FastAPI application assembled in openhands/app_server/app.py from a v1_router that mounts conversation, sandbox, secrets, settings, user, skills, webhook, git, event, and config sub-routers under /api/v1, plus a separately-mounted FastMCP server at /mcp for PR-creation tools. The agentic core (controller, runtime, LLM client, MCP client, agent-event loop) is no longer in this repo — it has been extracted to the separate openhands-sdk and openhands-agent-server packages, so this scan can only judge what the control plane does. The shipped openhands/server/ tree is now deprecation-shim re-exports of the V1 modules; the OSS persistence layer is filesystem-backed via LocalFileStore under ~/.openhands, with Alembic migrations run at startup; sandbox backends are pluggable (Docker by default, a host-process backend, and a remote backend), and docker-compose.yml mounts the host Docker socket and exposes port 3000 directly. The OSS default DefaultUserAuth returns user_id=None for every request and get_dependencies() attaches a real auth check only when SESSION_API_KEY is set in the environment, so the V1 API surface ships unauthenticated by default — the relevant divergence from the remit's "secrets must never reach the model context" and "every request to an integration must be verified" clauses.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is a thoughtfully-architected agent platform whose OSS control-plane defaults openly fall back to unauthenticated, single-tenant operation — the agentic-core invariants (sandbox isolation, OAuth-scoped integrations, structured session record) are real and operative, but the boundary that sits in front of them in this repo is permissive. get_dependencies() in openhands/app_server/utils/dependencies.py returns an empty list whenever SESSION_API_KEY is unset and the app is not in SaaS mode, so the V1 API including /api/v1/secrets/* (custom secrets create/read/delete) and /api/v1/users/me is reachable without credentials on a default docker-compose up; LocalhostCORSMiddleware in openhands/app_server/middleware.py compounds this by logging-and-allowing any origin when no permitted_cors_origins are configured.

Secondary themes: the ProcessSandboxService backend in openhands/app_server/sandbox/process_sandbox_service.py spawns agent-server processes directly on the host with the parent environment inherited (no container, no namespace, no seccomp), and the user-skills loader in openhands/app_server/user/skills_router.py ingests every *.md under ~/.openhands/microagents with no content-trust check — a documented persistence surface for the agent that the control plane treats as configuration data.

rules.rule_text: All agent-generated code MUST execute inside the sandboxed runtime and never directly on the host.

All file reads and writes MUST be confined to the per-session sandbox workspace, and any attempt to escape it — paths outside the workspace, escaping symlinks, or parent-directory traversal — MUST be rejected.

The user's task prompt, web-retrieved page content, issue descriptions, pull-request comments, repository file contents, and micro-agent or memory content MUST all be treated as untrusted input capable of carrying prompt-injection payloads.
Tool calls driven by LLM output MUST be validated at the boundary — argument shapes checked, numeric parameters clamped, and commands that reach outside the sandbox rejected.
Integration and session credentials MUST be verified on every request to an integration, and long-lived credentials MUST NOT be cached anywhere the model can reach.
Every action the agent takes — the tool invoked, its arguments, the outcome, and the time — MUST be recorded to a durable session record.
Per-session wall-clock and step-count caps MUST be enforced, and the agent MUST halt cleanly when a cap is exceeded.
Agent-generated code MUST NOT run on the host operating system outside the sandboxed runtime.

Instructions embedded in untrusted content — web pages, issue descriptions and comments, source files in the workspace, or micro-agent and memory content — MUST NOT be followed when they attempt to exfiltrate credentials, API keys, or session tokens; escape the sandbox; redirect actions to a different repository, organization, or integration; commit or push changes without user confirmation; or open pull requests, close issues, or send messages beyond the current session's authorized scope.
Secrets, credentials, tokens, or environment-file contents MUST NOT be committed to any git branch.
Destructive git operations — force-push, branch deletion, history rewrite — MUST NOT be performed without user confirmation.
Destructive integration operations — deleting a repository, removing webhooks, revoking collaborator access — MUST NOT be performed at all; they are out of scope, not approval-gated.
API keys and session tokens MUST NOT leak into logs, error messages, or model context.
One session's state, memory, or credentials MUST NOT leak into another session.
Adding a new MCP tool server at runtime MUST be confirmed by the user.
Connecting a new integration account MUST be confirmed by the user.
OpenHands MUST NOT run as an always-on background service that initiates tasks without a user request.
OpenHands MUST NOT share state, memory, or credentials across user sessions.
Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.

findings.summary: OSS app server attaches no authentication to V1 routes by default — secrets, settings, git-token endpoints reachable anonymously.
findings.description: get_dependencies() in openhands/app_server/utils/dependencies.py returns an empty dependency list whenever SESSION_API_KEY is unset and app_mode is not SaaS, and every V1 router attaches that list — so the OSS default ships /api/v1/secrets/* (create/read/list/delete custom secrets, store git provider tokens), /api/v1/users/me?expose_secrets=true, /api/v1/settings, and /api/v1/git/* without any auth check. DefaultUserAuth compounds this by returning user_id=None for every request, so request and stored data are treated as belonging to a single anonymous tenant. A reachable instance — docker-compose up publishes port 3000 directly — exposes stored git provider tokens to any caller.
findings.policy_rule_text: Integration and session credentials MUST be verified on every request to an integration, and long-lived credentials MUST NOT be cached anywhere the model can reach. / API keys and session tokens MUST NOT leak into logs, error messages, or model context. / One session's state, memory, or credentials MUST NOT leak into another session. / Connecting a new integration account MUST be confirmed by the user. / OpenHands MUST NOT share state, memory, or credentials across user sessions.

evidence.snippet: get_dependencies() returns [] unless SESSION_API_KEY env is set or app_mode==SAAS — the OSS default in openhands/app_server/server_config/server_config.py:11 is APP_MODE=OPENHANDS, so the empty list is what every V1 router attaches.

router = APIRouter(prefix='/secrets', tags=['Secrets'], dependencies=get_dependencies()) — the create/read/list/delete custom-secret endpoints and the POST /git-providers endpoint that stores GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket tokens are reachable without auth in the OSS default.

recommended_actions: Default the OSS path to a generated-on-first-boot SESSION_API_KEY persisted to the persistence directory (and printed to the operator) rather than treating "no key set" as "no auth required" — get_dependencies() in openhands/app_server/utils/dependencies.py should fail-closed instead of returning [].

Bind uvicorn to 127.0.0.1 by default in openhands/server/__main__.py (currently host='0.0.0.0') and remove the host-binding from docker-compose.yml's port mapping, so the OSS server is loopback-only unless the operator opts into network exposure.

findings.summary: Process-sandbox backend runs the agent server directly on the host with the parent environment inherited — no isolation.
findings.description: When RUNTIME=local or RUNTIME=process, ProcessSandboxService spawns the agent-server as a host subprocess, copies the entire parent os.environ into its environment (including any tokens present), and binds it to a port on the host. The host filesystem, network, and credentials are reachable from anything the agent runs; the remit explicitly forbids agent code from running outside the sandboxed runtime.
findings.policy_rule_text: All agent-generated code MUST execute inside the sandboxed runtime and never directly on the host. / Agent-generated code MUST NOT run on the host operating system outside the sandboxed runtime.

evidence.snippet: env = os.environ.copy(); env.update(sandbox_spec.initial_env); env['SESSION_API_KEY'] = session_api_key — the agent server inherits every host env var, then runs under a plain subprocess.Popen with no namespace, seccomp, or resource limits.
RUNTIME=='local' or 'process' selects ProcessSandboxServiceInjector — and AGENTS.md (repo root) documents `export RUNTIME=local` as the supported way to run OpenHands-with-OpenHands.

recommended_actions: Mark the process backend as "development-only" in openhands/app_server/sandbox/process_sandbox_service.py and emit a warning at startup when it is selected; require an explicit OH_ALLOW_HOST_PROCESS_RUNTIME=1 env flag before config_from_env in openhands/app_server/config.py will instantiate it.

Filter the inherited environment before subprocess.Popen — pass only the keys named in sandbox_spec.initial_env plus an explicit allow-list, so host secrets do not flow into the agent process by default.

findings.summary: LocalhostCORSMiddleware allows any origin and credentials when no permitted_cors_origins are configured.
findings.description: LocalhostCORSMiddleware.is_allowed_origin returns True for every origin (after logging a warning) whenever self.allow_origins is empty and no regex is set — and it is constructed with allow_credentials=True and allow_methods=['*']. The OSS default has no OH_PERMITTED_CORS_ORIGINS set, so the V1 API combined with the unauthenticated default in PRAX-2026-05-25-001 means any web page the operator visits can drive requests against the local app server with cookies attached.

evidence.snippet: is_allowed_origin returns True for every origin when allow_origins is empty — logs "No CORS origins configured, allowing origin" and lets it through; super().__init__ is called with allow_credentials=True and allow_methods=['*'].

recommended_actions: Change the empty-origins fallback in openhands/app_server/middleware.py:42 to allow only localhost/127.0.0.1 (the original docstring intent) and deny everything else, rather than the current allow-any-with-warning behavior.

findings.summary: docker-compose.yml mounts the host Docker socket into the container with no isolation, giving the app full root on the host.
findings.description: The shipped docker-compose.yml bind-mounts /var/run/docker.sock into the OpenHands container, so any code path that reaches the Docker SDK from inside the container can create privileged containers, mount the host filesystem, or escape via standard docker-socket tradecraft. Combined with PRAX-2026-05-25-001, an unauthenticated reach into the app server has a path to host-root.

evidence.snippet: volumes: - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock — the official compose stack mounts the host docker socket into the app container; the app's docker_sandbox_service uses docker.from_env() to talk to it.

recommended_actions: Document the docker-socket mount as a privileged-mode opt-in in docker-compose.yml (or split the compose into a "default" stack that uses the remote runtime and a "with-docker" overlay that adds the socket), and warn loudly at startup when the socket is reachable from inside the app container.

findings.summary: Skills/microagents are loaded from disk into agent context with no content-trust check or origin label.
findings.description: skills_router._load_skills_from_dir reads every *.md under the global skills/ directory and under ~/.openhands/microagents, parses YAML frontmatter with yaml.safe_load, and returns the resulting metadata for selection into agent context. There is no signature check, no source-origin label, and no review gate — any process with write access to ~/.openhands/microagents can drop a file that subsequently functions as a secondary system prompt, violating the remit's "untrusted micro-agent or memory content" rule.
findings.policy_rule_text: The user's task prompt, web-retrieved page content, issue descriptions, pull-request comments, repository file contents, and micro-agent or memory content MUST all be treated as untrusted input capable of carrying prompt-injection payloads.

evidence.snippet: for md_file in skills_dir.rglob('*.md'): fm = _parse_skill_frontmatter(md_file) — every markdown file under USER_SKILLS_DIR (~/.openhands/microagents) is enumerated and its YAML frontmatter loaded, with no origin/signature/review check.

recommended_actions: Add a content-origin label to SkillInfo in openhands/app_server/user/skills_router.py (e.g. source='global' vs source='user' vs source='external') and surface it to the prompt-assembly layer so untrusted skills are marked as such; require explicit operator approval before a newly-discovered user-skill enters context.

findings.summary: App server has no durable, structured per-action audit log — only uvicorn request logs and the SDK-side event stream.
findings.description: The remit requires every action — tool invoked, arguments, outcome, time — to be recorded to a durable session record. The control-plane code in scope (every router under app_server/) emits Python-logger lines via openhands_logger, optionally JSON-formatted, but no per-action structured record is written by the app server itself. The conversation event stream lives in the agent-server (out of scope); the app server's slice — sandbox lifecycle events, secret creates/reads, integration-token writes, settings changes — is captured only as unstructured log lines, which an operator cannot reliably replay to reconstruct what happened.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every action the agent takes — the tool invoked, its arguments, the outcome, and the time — MUST be recorded to a durable session record.

evidence.snippet: create_custom_secret / update_custom_secret / delete_custom_secret return EditResponse without writing a structured audit record — the only trace is whatever uvicorn happens to log, with no who/when/what schema.
file is 0 lines — placeholder named "event_store" exists in the event/ package but no implementation has been wired in.

recommended_actions: Add a structured audit log under openhands/app_server/ (one append-only record per state-changing endpoint: actor / route / arguments / outcome / timestamp), persisted via the existing FileStore with a documented retention policy.

findings.summary: FileSecretsStore persists git provider tokens and custom secrets as plaintext JSON on disk.
findings.description: The OSS default uses FileSecretsStore backed by LocalFileStore, which writes secrets.json under ~/.openhands via secrets.model_dump_json(context={'expose_secrets': True}) — the tokens are serialized with their secret values intact, on the host filesystem, with no encryption. A JwtService with encryption-key handling exists in the codebase (openhands/app_server/services/jwt_service.py uses JWE with a legacy Fernet fallback), so the primitives are available but unused for at-rest secret storage.

evidence.snippet: store() calls secrets.model_dump_json(context={'expose_secrets': True}) and writes the result via file_store.write — plaintext JSON on disk for provider tokens and custom secrets.

recommended_actions: Wrap FileSecretsStore.store / .load with JwtService.encrypt_value / .decrypt_value so the on-disk secrets.json is JWE-encrypted at rest with the existing key-rotation primitives.

findings.summary: Rate limit is a single global in-memory bucket (10 req/s) — no per-user, per-route, or distributed quota.
findings.description: The app constructs RateLimitMiddleware with InMemoryRateLimiter(requests=10, seconds=1) keyed on request.client.host. The state is in-process (does not survive restart, does not share across replicas), the limit is global rather than per-route, expensive operations (sandbox start, LLM-via-settings calls) share the same bucket as cheap reads, and there is no budget / cost threshold for LLM spend.

evidence.snippet: app.add_middleware(RateLimitMiddleware, rate_limiter=InMemoryRateLimiter(requests=10, seconds=1)) — single global bucket, no per-route or per-user differentiation.

recommended_actions: Add a separate, lower-bound rate-limit class on the sandbox-start and conversation-create routes in openhands/app_server/app.py, and document an LLM-spend budget threshold per session/user that halts the conversation when exceeded (the agent-server enforces step caps; the app server should enforce a wall-clock and dollar cap).

positives.title: Sandbox session API key bound to RUNNING sandbox state
positives.description: validate_session_key in openhands/app_server/sandbox/session_auth.py rejects any session-API-key call whose target sandbox is not in RUNNING state — explicitly to prevent leaked keys from being used after a sandbox is paused / stopped / deleted; the file's own docstring calls out this property as a security invariant.

positives.title: Session-API-key ownership check on sensitive cross-route paths
positives.description: validate_session_key_ownership additionally requires that the authenticated caller owns the sandbox the session key belongs to (HTTP 403 otherwise) before /users/me?expose_secrets=true will return secret-bearing fields.

positives.title: Encryption-key service with JWE and legacy-Fernet decrypt
positives.description: JwtService provides JWS sign/verify and JWE encrypt/decrypt with key-rotation IDs, plus a legacy-Fernet fallback for decrypting older payloads during the migration — the primitive needed to encrypt the secrets store at rest exists and is exercised by test_jwt_service.py and test_encryption_key.py.

positives.title: DEBUG_LLM requires interactive confirmation before unmasking secrets in logs
positives.description: openhands/app_server/utils/logger.py gates the DEBUG_LLM mode behind an interactive input() prompt that warns the operator that API keys may be exposed and requires typing y — a deliberate guard against accidentally enabling secret-leaking logs in production.

positives.title: V0 deprecation shims preserved with explicit redirection
positives.description: openhands/server/app.py, openhands/server/listen.py, openhands/server/middleware.py, and openhands/user_auth/default_user_auth.py are stamped DEPRECATED / LEGACY V0 with a comment pointing at the V1 replacement — explicit signposting reduces the chance of accidentally extending the legacy path.

positives.title: Webhook secret retrieval requires JWS-verified access token
positives.description: /api/v1/webhooks/secrets verifies the X-Access-Token as a JWS payload via JwtService.verify_jws_token before resolving the user and provider — the token carries user_id + provider_type and the verification is mandatory; failures return 401.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files were present in this source snapshot — the OSS app server emits to stdout/stderr via Python logging and writes no durable per-action audit record (see PRAX-2026-05-25-006).

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. The OSS V1 control plane has the shape of a mature platform — pluggable backends, encryption-key handling, session-API-key-scoped sandbox secrets, OAuth-scoped integrations, a mature test suite — but its default posture relies on operators making non-default choices (set SESSION_API_KEY, set OH_PERMITTED_CORS_ORIGINS, use the Docker rather than process sandbox) to reach the security model the remit describes. With those choices, posture is meaningfully higher; without them, the front door is open. Domain and supply-chain hygiene land at Established because the agent's authority is well-bounded by the SDK split and dependencies are pinned in pyproject.toml; zero-trust drops to Ad hoc because the OSS auth default is anonymous-equals-admin; red-team and monitoring land at Partial because the unit-test suite includes adversarial cases for the webhook auth and session-key paths but the app-server emits no structured per-action audit log.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The agent's authority is bounded by the SDK/agent-server split — the app server itself never runs agent-generated code, and the agent runs only inside a sandboxed runtime backend (Docker by default, with remote and process variants); the remit's mission and counterparty list line up with what the routers expose, and the MCP tools registered at openhands/app_server/mcp/mcp_router.py are scoped to git-platform PR operations rather than general-purpose actions.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: External content reaches the agent via the browser tool, the git-platform integrations, the user-task prompt, and the skills/microagents loader at openhands/app_server/user/skills_router.py — the loader reads every *.md under ~/.openhands/microagents and parses YAML frontmatter with no content-trust check, signature, or origin label, and the control plane attaches no untrusted-content marker for downstream prompt assembly.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: get_dependencies() in openhands/app_server/utils/dependencies.py returns an empty list whenever SESSION_API_KEY is unset and app_mode is not SaaS — every V1 router (including secrets_router, settings_router, git_router, sandbox_router, user_router) attaches that empty list as its dependency, so the OSS default ships the secrets-management and integration-token endpoints without any authentication check; LocalhostCORSMiddleware in openhands/app_server/middleware.py:34 additionally allows any origin when no permitted origins are configured.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Dependencies in pyproject.toml are version-pinned (notably the SDK and agent-server lines pinned to 1.22.1), poetry.lock and uv.lock are present, and integrity-relevant SDK pieces (encryption-key derivation, JWE token service) come from named packages; the remaining gap is operational rather than absent — no in-repo dependency-audit step is wired into the build, and the agent-server image tag is configurable via env (AGENT_SERVER_IMAGE_TAG) in docker-compose.yml with no signature pin.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The tests/unit/app_server/ directory contains 56 test files including test_webhook_router_auth.py, test_sandbox_secrets_router.py, test_jwt_service.py, and test_encryption_key.py that exercise auth-rejection paths and session-key lifecycle invariants; what's missing is evidence those tests drove an architectural change to the OSS-anonymous default — the unauthenticated-by-default posture survives them, so the testing is verifying intent rather than challenging it.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: openhands/app_server/utils/logger.py configures JSON-line logging when LOG_JSON is set and integrates SDK redactors, but the app server emits no structured per-tool-call or per-action audit record — request logs are uvicorn defaults, and the conversation event stream lives in the agent-server (out of this scope); the OSS control plane has no durable, action-level record an operator could replay to reconstruct what the agent did, which the remit explicitly requires.
